1

Environmental Conditions Across CHCCS Schools

CHCCS faces the difficult decision of closing schools. The Board has identified criteria including facilities condition, enrollment trends, and community impact. But there are other factors that should inform this decision: environmental and geographic conditions that affect student health and long-term infrastructure viability.

Only three schools are being considered for closure, which creates an uncomfortable comparison. Though this analysis is Ephesus-focused, we want to state clearly: no school community should ever be permanently closed. Ephesus, Glenwood, and Seawell are all amazing schools.

Now, we start by analyzing the environmental and geographic conditions.

2

What We Analyze

We examine three environmental factors across all 11 elementary schools:

  • Flood risk — FEMA flood zone overlap with school properties
  • Traffic air pollution (TRAP) — modeled exposure from nearby roads
  • Urban heat island (UHI) — heat vulnerability from surrounding land cover

These factors affect student health, infrastructure costs, and long-term viability of school sites.

Full interactive map: CHCCS Environmental Analysis • Methodology: Environmental Methodology
3

What Are Flood Zones?

FEMA classifies areas by flood risk. A 100-year flood zone has a 1% annual chance of flooding; a 500-year flood zone has a 0.2% annual chance.

These sound rare — but they are not. A property in a 100-year flood zone has a 26% chance of flooding over a 30-year mortgage. A 500-year zone still carries meaningful cumulative risk.

The map shows FEMA flood zones across the CHCCS district.

Data: FEMA National Flood Hazard Layer (NFHL)
4

Chapel Hill’s Flood History

Hurricane Chantal (2025) produced a 1,000-year rainfall event with extensive flooding throughout Chapel Hill, causing an estimated $54 million in total damage to homes, businesses, and public property — including $20.7 million in commercial damage and $22.6 million in public damage. Most significant damage occurred in areas of known flood risk.

Hurricane Fran (1996) and Hurricane Floyd (1999) each brought major flooding to the region, with some estimates suggesting rainfall intensities approaching or exceeding 500-year levels in parts of central North Carolina.

That is at least three severe flood events in roughly 30 years — approximately one per decade. Climate change projections suggest this trend will intensify.

A “500-year flood” is not a once-in-a-lifetime event in Chapel Hill.

5

Schools at Risk: FPG & Rashkis

Only 2 of 11 schools have FEMA flood zone overlap on their property. Percentages below represent the portion of each school’s property that falls within the referenced flood plain:

100-year flood zone
(1.22 acres)
4.8%

500-year flood zone

Neither is being considered for closure, but both represent assets at ongoing flood risk.

6

FPG Relocation & the Liability Question

FPG is planned to rebuild on a new site. Once relocated, the current property — with over a quarter of its acreage in a 100-year flood zone — becomes a liability.

If FPG is to be relocated, is there any good rationale for keeping a property that is at documented risk of flooding?

7

Climate Resilience of Closure Candidates

All three schools under consideration for closure — Ephesus, Glenwood, and Seawell — have zero flood zone overlap.

These are climate-resilient sites. Perhaps schools with greater climate resilience should be maintained.

Key finding: The only schools with flood exposure (FPG and Rashkis) are not on the closure list. All three closure candidates are on flood-safe ground.
8

Traffic Air Pollution & Children’s Health

Traffic-related air pollution (TRAP) — nitrogen dioxide, particulate matter, ultrafine particles — is directly linked to adverse health outcomes in children including asthma, reduced lung function, and impaired cognitive development.

Studies show children at schools near major roads have higher rates of respiratory illness and lower academic performance.

References: EPA Integrated Science Assessment for NO₂ (2016) • HEI Traffic-Related Air Pollution (2010) • Sunyer et al., PLOS Medicine (2015) • California Air Resources Board school proximity studies
9

Policies Protecting Children from TRAP

A growing number of jurisdictions prohibit siting schools near major roads:

  • California (SB 352, 2025): Prohibits school site approval within 500 feet of a freeway or busy traffic corridor unless the district proves air-quality mitigation will protect students.
  • New York (The SIGH Act, 2024): Prevents new schools within 500 feet of a major highway.
  • Federal (EPA guidance): Recommends 500–1,000 foot buffers from major highways and distribution centers.

The trend is toward more restrictive siting rules. Reopening or expanding schools on sites with high traffic exposure may face increasing regulatory and liability scrutiny in the years ahead.

Chapel Hill has no such policy, but the data is clear: proximity to traffic matters for children’s health and academic outcomes.

10

TRAP Methodology (Brief)

We modeled TRAP exposure using road network data from OpenStreetMap, NCDOT traffic counts, and an exponential decay function (pollution drops ~50% within 230m of a road). Tree canopy mitigation reduces exposure by up to 56% per published literature.

Full methodology: Environmental Methodology Walkthrough
11

Raw Traffic Pollution

Raw traffic pollution index across the district. Hotter colors indicate higher cumulative exposure from nearby roads.

Major corridors (I-40, US 15-501, MLK Blvd) create clear pollution corridors that extend into school neighborhoods.

12

Net Pollution (After Tree Canopy)

After accounting for tree canopy mitigation, some areas see significant reduction. Mature tree cover absorbs and disperses pollutants.

However, schools near major roads remain elevated even with canopy. Trees help, but they cannot fully compensate for proximity to heavy traffic.

13

Traffic Pollution: All Schools Compared

Net TRAP exposure (500m radius, normalized 0–100) for all schools, including FPG’s planned new location:

Glenwood has the highest net TRAP exposure of any CHCCS elementary school — bordered by Estes Drive and near US 15-501. Its location directly adjacent to major roadways makes it fundamentally unsuitable as a long-term elementary school site.

FPG’s current site ranks #2, similarly compromised by its proximity to major roads. Its new planned location drops to #9 — a dramatic improvement that underscores how much site selection matters for children’s health.

Ephesus (12.8) and Seawell (3.9) have relatively low exposure.

14

What Is Urban Heat?

Urban heat islands form where pavement and buildings absorb and re-radiate heat, while tree canopy provides cooling.

We estimated heat vulnerability using ESA WorldCover land classification — a proxy based on surrounding land cover, not direct temperature measurement. Higher scores indicate more built-up, less vegetated surroundings.

Why this matters for elementary schools: Children are more vulnerable to heat stress than adults — their smaller body mass, higher metabolic rate, and less developed thermoregulation make them susceptible to heat-related illness. Extreme heat reduces outdoor recess and physical activity time, and research links elevated temperatures to decreased concentration and academic performance. Schools in high-UHI areas also face higher cooling costs and greater risk of heat-related illness during arrival, dismissal, and outdoor activities.

Limitation: This is a land-cover proxy, not a direct temperature measurement. It indicates relative heat vulnerability, not actual temperature differences.
15

Urban Heat Vulnerability Map

The UHI proxy reveals a clear pattern: downtown and commercial corridors are hotter; forested areas along stream buffers are cooler.

Blue indicates cooler (more vegetation), red indicates hotter (more impervious surface).

Urban heat matters for elementary schools because children are more vulnerable to heat stress than adults — their smaller body mass and less developed thermoregulation increase the risk of heat-related illness. High-UHI school sites mean reduced outdoor recess time on hot days, higher cooling costs, and greater health risk during arrival and dismissal.

16

Urban Heat: All Schools Compared

UHI proxy scores (500m radius) for all schools:

Glenwood again ranks near the top (#2) for heat vulnerability. FPG’s current site is #3.

Among the closure candidates, Ephesus (14.7) is moderate, while Seawell (11.5) benefits from more surrounding vegetation.

17

Environmental Summary

Flood Risk

Only FPG and Rashkis have flood zone overlap. All 3 closure candidates have zero flood exposure. FPG’s current site is a flood liability that should not be maintained post-relocation.

Traffic Pollution

Glenwood and FPG (current) are far above all other schools. FPG’s new site would dramatically improve. Ephesus and Seawell have low exposure.

Urban Heat

Glenwood and FPG again rank highest. Ephesus is moderate; Seawell is low.

Bottom line: Environmental data alone does not determine which school should close — but it should inform the conversation.
18

What’s Next

Environmental and geographic conditions are just one lens. Next, we examine Chapel Hill’s demographics — the people, communities, and socioeconomic patterns that define each school’s role in the district.
